name: Central core layout
description: Floor-layout strategy in which a single compact core at the geometric centre of the footprint absorbs all vertical circulation (staircase, ladder shaft or lift well) and the main horizontal hub, while the rest of the rooms radiate outward against the perimeter walls. The core typically occupies 15-25% of the floor area and acts as the spine that ties every level together; the surrounding 3-6 peripheral rooms each get one or two external facades for natural light. This pattern is the workhorse of vertical houses and towers (where stacking demands a fixed stair position on every floor), of compact modern apartments where a service core hides plumbing and ducts, and of minimalist plans that want every habitable room pushed to the daylit edge.
placement_rules: Place the core so its centroid coincides with the geometric centre of the floor footprint; offsets break the radial symmetry that makes the layout legible.
Size the core between 15% and 25% of the floor area — below that the stair plus a one-block landing will not fit, above that the peripheral rooms lose depth.
Stack the core identically on every floor so the staircase rises straight through the building without jogs or transfer slabs.
Distribute 3 to 6 peripheral rooms around the core; fewer leaves dead corners, more produces slivers too narrow to furnish.
Every peripheral room must touch at least one external wall so it gets a window; only the core itself is allowed to be fully interior.
Door of each peripheral room opens onto the core's circulation ring, never onto another room, so the core is the single hub.
Reserve at least 1 block of clearance between the core wall and the surrounding rooms on every side to form a continuous circulation ring.
